content.text: Rewriting the plan file with the Branch 2 pivot and all eight revisions.

input.content:
---
type: implementation-plan
created: 2026-04-11
revised: 2026-04-11 (after advanced-elicitation pass: pivoted to shared/finish-menu.md)
topic: Consistent "[C] Finish" menu + health-check hint across all 14 SKF workflows
status: draft — awaiting user approval at Phase 1 decision gate
---

# Plan: Consistent "Finish" Menu + Health-Check Hint (14 Workflows)

## Goal

The user saw this prompt in a `skf-test-skill` session (screenshot evidence):

> **[C] Finish** — press C (or say "continue") to run the shared health check and close out the workflow.

They want every SKF workflow (all 14) to present the same consistent gate with the same explicit hint before chaining into `shared/health-check.md`. Today, only 3 of the 14 terminal steps present any user-facing menu at all (`skf-test-skill`, `skf-refine-architecture`, `skf-verify-stack`), none of those 3 *name* the health check in the menu string, and the hint the user saw was improvised by the agent at run time — not baked into any step file.

### Two deliverables

1. **UX consistency** — a single canonical finish menu with the explicit health-check hint, applied to all 14 terminal steps via a shared file (not 14 duplicated copies).
2. **Verification guarantee** — confirm every workflow still chains correctly into `shared/health-check.md` (they all do today, just need to not regress).

### Why this plan is shaped around a shared file, not 14 per-file edits

The v1 draft of this plan proposed inserting a canonical 18-line menu block into each of 14 terminal steps. An advanced-elicitation pass (pre-mortem + first-principles + tree-of-thoughts, scored) showed that shape had a 4/14 weighted score; a shared-file shape scored 8.5/14. The key findings that triggered the pivot:

- **Duplication footgun.** 14 copies of the hint sentence drift over time. The plan itself flagged drift as a "follow-up", but shipping a known footgun is unacceptable.
- **Localization surface area.** 14 copies × N languages = N × 14 translation points. The shared-file shape collapses this to N.
- **Testing surface area.** 14 new gates to test for `{headless_mode}` propagation (the #1 pre-mortem failure mode) collapses to 1 gate.
- **Rollback cost.** 14 body-insertion reverts collapses to 14 one-line frontmatter reverts + 1 file delete.

---

## Phase 0 — Documentation Discovery (DONE)

### The 14 terminal step files

All set `nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'` in frontmatter today. No other files chain to the health check.

| # | Workflow | Terminal step file | Current finish-UX |
|---|----------|--------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | skf-analyze-source | `steps-c/step-06-generate-briefs.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 2 | skf-audit-skill | `steps-c/step-06-report.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 3 | skf-brief-skill | `steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 4 | skf-create-skill | `steps-c/step-08-report.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 5 | skf-create-stack-skill | `steps-c/step-09-report.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 6 | skf-drop-skill | `steps-c/step-03-report.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 7 | skf-export-skill | `steps-c/step-06-summary.md` | NO menu — auto-chains (mentions health-check-as-terminal in internal text) |
| 8 | skf-quick-skill | `steps-c/step-06-write.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 9 | skf-refine-architecture | `steps-c/step-06-report.md` | YES — `[R] Review changes in detail \| [X] Exit refinement` |
| 10 | skf-rename-skill | `steps-c/step-03-report.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 11 | skf-setup | `steps-c/step-04-report.md` | NO menu — auto-chains |
| 12 | skf-test-skill | `steps-c/step-06-report.md` | YES — `[C] Finish` (bare, no hint) |
| 13 | skf-update-skill | `steps-c/step-07-report.md` | NO menu — auto-chains (mentions health-check-as-terminal in internal text) |
| 14 | skf-verify-stack | `steps-c/step-06-report.md` | YES — `[R] Review full report \| [X] Exit verification` |

### Relevant shared file

`src/shared/health-check.md` — the terminal step. Must remain reusable from any caller. It:
- Has no `nextStepFile` (truly terminal)
- Silent-exits clean runs: `"Health Check: Clean run. No workflow issues to report. Workflow complete."`
- Has its own `[Y]/[N]/[E]` gate only if findings exist

### Precedent: `{headless_mode}` gate pattern

`skf-test-skill` line 155 already uses:

```markdown
**GATE [default: C]** — If `{headless_mode}`: auto-proceed with [C] Continue, log: "headless: auto-continue past report menu"
```

This is the pattern the new shared file will adopt. **Pre-condition:** the `{headless_mode}` variable must propagate from `skf-forger` (or any orchestrator) into sub-workflows. Phase 0.5 tests this empirically before any file edits.

### Path-resolution convention

`src/shared/health-check.md:11` documents: `shared/` paths resolve against the SKF module root (`_bmad/skf/` when installed, `src/` during development), **not** against the calling step file. This means a two-hop chain (caller → `shared/finish-menu.md` → `shared/health-check.md`) should work the same as a one-hop chain. Phase 0.5 verifies this empirically.

### Anti-patterns to avoid

- ❌ Do not edit `src/shared/health-check.md` itself. The hint lives in the *calling path*, not in the terminal.
- ❌ Do not break `nextStepFile` resolution. Changing 14 frontmatter entries is safe *only* if the two-hop chain is verified first.
- ❌ Do not invent new variables. Use existing ones: `{nextStepFile}`, `{communication_language}`, `{headless_mode}`.
- ❌ Do not delete the `[R] Review` branch in refine-architecture / verify-stack. It's a legitimate second option, not a duplicate of the finish gate.
- ❌ Do not assume the health check emits a "workflow complete" banner on every path. It doesn't on the clean-run path.
- ❌ Do not localize menu letters. `[C]`, `[R]`, `Finish` stay English; surrounding narration follows `{communication_language}`.

---

## Phase 0.5 — Pre-flight checks (NEW, runs before any edits)

### 0.5.1 — Verify `{headless_mode}` propagates through `skf-forger`

`skf-forger` chains multiple sub-workflows. We need empirical proof that when forger runs in headless mode, the `{headless_mode}` variable is visible to sub-workflow step files (including the new shared/finish-menu.md we're about to write).

**Test:**
1. Pick a fixture skill that takes `skf-forger` through at least one sub-workflow that already has a `{headless_mode}`-gated menu (`skf-test-skill` qualifies).
2. Run `skf-forger` headlessly against the fixture.
3. Observe: does `skf-test-skill` step-06-report.md's `[C] Finish` menu auto-continue (current behavior expected) or stall?

**If it auto-continues:** propagation works → proceed with the plan.

**If it stalls:** Phase 2 is blocked until a prior fix lands to propagate `{headless_mode}` into sub-workflows. File a blocking issue; do not continue this plan.

### 0.5.2 — Verify two-hop `shared/` path resolution

The plan adds a new intermediate shared step: `shared/finish-menu.md` whose own frontmatter sets `nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'`. This creates a two-hop chain:

```
caller/step-NN.md  →  shared/finish-menu.md  →  shared/health-check.md
```

`src/shared/health-check.md:11` *implies* two-hop works (paths resolve against module root, not calling step), but the codebase has zero existing examples of a two-hop `shared/` chain. This is untested territory.

**Test:**
1. Create a throwaway scratch file at `src/shared/finish-menu.md` with dummy body + `nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'`.
2. Temporarily point *one* caller (suggest `skf-drop-skill/steps-c/step-03-report.md` — the smallest file, easiest to revert) at `shared/finish-menu.md`.
3. Run `skf-drop-skill` against a fixture skill.
4. Observe: does the chain flow through both hops correctly? Does the health check run? Does `shared/finish-menu.md` resolve against module root in both dev (`src/`) and installed (`_bmad/skf/`) layouts?

**If it works:** proceed with Phase 2 and formalize the throwaway `shared/finish-menu.md` into the real one.

**If it breaks:** fall back to Branch 3 (hint inside `shared/health-check.md` header) — see Phase 1 Decision 3 below.

### 0.5.3 — Re-inventory `nextStepFile` references

Even though Phase 0 inventoried 14 files today, a new terminal step may have been added since plan-drafting. Run:

```bash
rg -l "^nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'" src/
```

Compare against the Phase 0 inventory. If the count is not exactly 14, update the plan before editing.

---

## Phase 1 — Decision gate (STOP — get user confirmation before editing)

### Decision 1: Canonical menu content for `shared/finish-menu.md`

Proposed body (written in user-experience language, not systems-speak):

````markdown
---
name: 'finish-menu'
description: 'Shared terminal-prelude menu — gates workflow exit before the health check'
nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
---

# Finish Menu

> **Path convention:** This file is referenced as `shared/finish-menu.md` from workflow step frontmatter. All `shared/` paths resolve relative to the SKF module root (`_bmad/skf/` when installed, `src/` during development), not relative to the calling step file.

## STEP GOAL

Present a consistent "finish" gate before chaining into the workflow self-improvement health check. Give the user an explicit beat to ask questions about the preceding report, then run the health check.

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES

- Narrate surrounding context in `{communication_language}`.
- **Display the menu block (`[C] Finish`, key letters, and the hint sentence) in English**, regardless of communication language. Menu keys must not be translated.

## MANDATORY SEQUENCE

### 1. Display Finish Menu

Display (narration in `{communication_language}`, menu block in English):

"**[C] Finish** — Press C to finish and I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out."

### 2. Menu Handling Logic

- **IF `C`:** Load and execute `{nextStepFile}` (the shared health check).
- **IF any other input:** Answer the user's follow-up question about the preceding report, then redisplay the menu.

### 3. Execution Rules

- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after displaying the menu.
- **GATE [default: C]** — If `{headless_mode}`: auto-proceed with `[C]`, log: "headless: auto-continue past finish menu".
- **GATE [skip]** — If `{skip_finish_gate}` is set to `true` by the caller: bypass the menu entirely and load `{nextStepFile}` immediately. (Reserved for workflows that already presented a substantive gate upstream and don't need a second one.)
- The user may ask follow-up questions about the preceding report — answer them, then redisplay the menu.

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

This is a **prelude** step, not the terminal step. After `[C]` is selected (or auto-selected), this step chains to `shared/health-check.md`, which is the true workflow terminal.
````

**User questions to confirm:**
- Is the hint sentence wording right? ("Press C to finish and I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out.")
- Is the user-experience framing preferred over the systems-speak version ("run the shared health check and close out the workflow")? The elicitation pass argued yes; confirm.

### Decision 2: Scope — which workflows get a finish gate at all?

Two options:

- **Option 2a (recommended):** All 14 workflows redirect their `nextStepFile` to `shared/finish-menu.md`. The `{headless_mode}` gate covers non-interactive runs. Friction cost for short workflows (skf-rename-skill, skf-drop-skill, skf-setup) is one keystroke in interactive mode.
- **Option 2b:** Only workflows that produced a substantive artifact to review get the gate. The short mechanical workflows (skf-rename-skill, skf-setup, skf-drop-skill) keep their current auto-chain directly to `shared/health-check.md`. Trade-off: inconsistency, but lower friction for quick ops.

Orchestrator recommends 2a for consistency — the interactive cost is one keystroke, and `shared/finish-menu.md` handles any future "skip this gate" cases via the `{skip_finish_gate}` variable.

### Decision 3: How to handle `skf-refine-architecture` and `skf-verify-stack` (already have `[R] Review | [X] Exit` menus)

Three options:

- **Option 3a:** Redirect `nextStepFile` to `shared/finish-menu.md`. The existing `[R]/[X]` menu in the step file stays as-is — the user picks `[X]`, the step chains via `nextStepFile` to `shared/finish-menu.md`, which then shows `[C] Finish`. **Trade-off:** user sees two finish prompts back-to-back. The `[R]/[X]` menu asks "review more or move on?"; the `[C]` menu asks "ready to finish?". Arguably different questions, but it *feels* redundant.
- **Option 3b:** Redirect `nextStepFile` to `shared/finish-menu.md` AND set `{skip_finish_gate}=true` in the caller. The existing `[R]/[X]` menu serves as the finish gate; `shared/finish-menu.md` silently chains through. The hint sentence appears inline in the `[X]` line of refine-architecture and verify-stack. Trade-off: the hint sentence now lives in 3 files (shared/finish-menu.md + 2 callers), not 1. Still an 11× reduction from 14, but not pure DRY.
- **Option 3c:** Remove the `[R] Review` branch entirely from those two files. `[R]` behavior moves upstream (into a different step), and the terminal step just auto-chains to `shared/finish-menu.md`. Trade-off: structural change to two workflows, touches more than just the finish-UX.

Orchestrator recommends **3b** — it preserves the existing UX (one menu per workflow), the hint sentence still lives mostly in one place, and the `{skip_finish_gate}` mechanism becomes a reusable primitive for any future workflow that needs to opt out of the finish gate.

### Decision 4: `[X]` legacy alias for refine-architecture / verify-stack

Under option 3b, the `[X]` letter stays in refine-architecture and verify-stack (we don't rename it to `[C]`). **No alias work needed** — `[X]` keeps its current meaning. This resolves the muscle-memory concern flagged in the pre-mortem without any special handling.

(If the user prefers option 3a, a legacy `[X]→[C]` alias *is* needed. Flag and confirm.)

### Decision 5: Should headless mode run the health check at all?

First-principles challenge from the elicitation pass: the health check exists for *human reflection on friction*. A headless agent cannot reflect on its own friction honestly — it can only fabricate findings or report "clean run". Arguably, headless mode should **skip the health check entirely**, not auto-continue through it.

**Orchestrator recommendation:** out of scope for this plan. Keep the current behavior (headless auto-continues into the health check, which then runs its clean-run-exit path). Flag as a separate follow-up — "Should headless mode skip the health check entirely?" — and address it independently.

### Decision 6: Risk-acceptance for the two-hop path

If Phase 0.5.2 reveals that two-hop `shared/` resolution breaks: fall back to Branch 3 (put the hint inside `shared/health-check.md`'s header; accept that the hint lands one beat later than the user's screenshot showed). **Confirm the user is OK with this fallback** before Phase 0.5 runs, so we don't stall at a decision mid-execution.

**HALT Phase 1. Do not proceed to Phase 2 until the user confirms Decisions 1–6.**

---

## Phase 2 — Create shared file + redirect 14 `nextStepFile` entries

### Files to touch

1. **NEW:** `src/shared/finish-menu.md` — canonical body from Phase 1 Decision 1.
2. **FRONTMATTER EDIT (one-line):** all 14 terminal step files from the Phase 0 inventory. Change `nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'` → `nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'`.

For the two workflows handled under Decision 3b (refine-architecture + verify-stack): *also* add `skip_finish_gate: true` to the frontmatter, and add the hint sentence inline to the `[X]` line in the body. (If Decision 3a is chosen instead, skip the `skip_finish_gate` frontmatter.)

### Per-file edit recipe

**For the 12 auto-chaining + test-skill files:**

1. Read the file header (first 20 lines).
2. Edit only the `nextStepFile` frontmatter line:
   ```diff
   - nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
   + nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'
   ```
3. Update the frontmatter comment above it:
   ```diff
   - # nextStepFile `shared/health-check.md` resolves relative to the SKF module
   + # nextStepFile `shared/finish-menu.md` resolves relative to the SKF module
   ```
4. **For skf-test-skill only:** delete the inline `### 7. Present MENU OPTIONS` section (lines ~143–157). The shared finish menu now owns this UX.

**For refine-architecture and verify-stack (Decision 3b):**

1. Edit frontmatter:
   ```diff
   - nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
   + nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'
   ```
2. Add to frontmatter: `skip_finish_gate: true`.
3. Edit the `[X] Exit` menu line to append the hint sentence:
   ```diff
   - **[X] Exit refinement** (or **[X] Exit verification**)
   + **[X] Exit refinement** — I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out.
   ```

### Anti-pattern guards

- ❌ Do not edit the body of files 1–11 or 13 (the auto-chaining ones). The frontmatter change is sufficient — `shared/finish-menu.md` handles the UX.
- ❌ Do not forget the `# nextStepFile` comment update. The comment references the path as part of the workflow-author documentation; leaving it stale creates future confusion.
- ❌ Do not set `skip_finish_gate: true` on any file except refine-architecture and verify-stack. The 12 others need the gate.
- ❌ Do not change any `nextStepFile` reference that points anywhere else (e.g. `steps-c/step-05-*.md` internal chains). Only the 14 terminal `shared/health-check.md` references.

### Mid-phase verification

Run after the new shared file is created but before touching caller frontmatter:

```bash
# New file exists and has valid frontmatter
ls -la src/shared/finish-menu.md
rg "^nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'$" src/shared/finish-menu.md
```

Run after each caller edit:

```bash
# Target file now points at finish-menu
rg -n "^nextStepFile:" src/skf-{workflow}/steps-c/*.md
```

---

## Phase 3 — (Collapsed into Phase 2 under the shared-file pivot)

The v1 plan had a separate Phase 3 for "reconcile 3 files with existing menus". Under Branch 2, those three files are handled inline in Phase 2 alongside the other 11 — there's no separate reconcile step. Phase 3 is empty.

---

## Phase 4 — Verification (final sweep)

Run from repo root. All must pass.

### 4.1 — All 14 callers point at `shared/finish-menu.md`

```bash
rg -c "^nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'" src/skf-*/steps-c/*.md
```

Expected: exactly 14 files, each with `1`.

### 4.2 — Only ONE file points at `shared/health-check.md` (the new shared/finish-menu.md)

```bash
rg -l "^nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'" src/
```

Expected: exactly one path: `src/shared/finish-menu.md`.

### 4.3 — The new shared file exists and has the canonical hint

```bash
rg "quick check for any friction" src/shared/finish-menu.md
```

Expected: at least one hit.

### 4.4 — `skip_finish_gate` only on the two intended files

```bash
rg -l "^skip_finish_gate: true" src/
```

Expected: exactly two paths — `src/skf-refine-architecture/steps-c/step-06-report.md` and `src/skf-verify-stack/steps-c/step-06-report.md`.

### 4.5 — Hint sentence appears inline in the two Decision-3b files

```bash
rg -l "quick check for any friction" src/skf-refine-architecture/ src/skf-verify-stack/
```

Expected: one file each (the terminal step).

### 4.6 — Headless gate present in `shared/finish-menu.md`

```bash
rg "GATE \[default: C\]" src/shared/finish-menu.md
```

Expected: at least one hit.

### 4.7 — `src/shared/health-check.md` is untouched

```bash
git diff --stat src/shared/health-check.md
```

Expected: no diff.

### 4.8 — Build succeeds

```bash
npm run build 2>&1 | tail -40
```

Expected: success, no frontmatter parse errors on any of the 15 changed files.

### 4.9 — End-to-end smoke tests (manual)

- **Interactive run:** `skf-test-skill` against a fixture → finish menu appears, `C` chains into health check, clean-run path exits silently.
- **Headless run:** `skf-forger` against a fixture → every sub-workflow's finish menu auto-continues without stalling.
- **Two-hop resolution:** `skf-drop-skill` against a fixture → confirms the chain `caller → finish-menu → health-check` works end-to-end in the installed layout (not just in-repo dev layout). If possible, test against a freshly-installed `_bmad/skf/` copy, not just `src/`.
- **`skip_finish_gate` behavior:** `skf-refine-architecture` against a fixture → the `[R]/[X]` menu appears, selecting `[X]` chains silently through `shared/finish-menu.md` (no second gate) and into the health check.
- **Language smoke test:** run one workflow with `{communication_language}` set to French (or Japanese) → confirm narration is translated but the `[C] Finish` menu block and hint sentence remain English.

---

## Risks / open questions

1. **Two-hop `shared/` resolution is untested in the codebase.** Phase 0.5.2 is the explicit de-risk. If it fails, Branch 3 (hint inside health-check.md header) is the fallback — Decision 6 pre-commits the user to this fallback so Phase 2 doesn't stall.
2. **3-place near-DRY under Decision 3b.** The hint sentence lives in `shared/finish-menu.md` + refine-architecture's `[X]` line + verify-stack's `[X]` line = 3 copies. Better than 14, worse than 1. Acceptable if documented; flagged for future refactor when `{workflow_label}` variable becomes available.
4. **`skf-forger` headless propagation.** Phase 0.5.1 de-risks this explicitly before any file edits.
5. **Localization key-preservation.** The canonical template hard-wires "menu block in English, narration in `{communication_language}`". This must be followed rigorously at render time. Phase 4.9 includes a language smoke test.
6. **Headless mode should maybe skip the health check entirely.** First-principles challenge — out of scope here, flagged as follow-up.
7. **If the `{skip_finish_gate}` variable doesn't yet exist in the SKF variable system**, Phase 2 needs a prior micro-phase to wire it in. Verify before Phase 2 starts: grep for how `{headless_mode}` is defined/propagated; add `{skip_finish_gate}` alongside it using the same mechanism.
8. **The v2 plan collapses Phase 3.** Future readers skimming the plan may be confused by an empty Phase 3 header. Left in place as a documentation breadcrumb — flags that the v1 plan had structural phases that the elicitation pass merged.

---

## Execution order recap

1. **Phase 0.5 — pre-flight checks.** Headless propagation test + two-hop resolution test + re-inventory. HALT on any failure.
2. **Phase 1 — decision gate.** Confirm Decisions 1–6 with the user. HALT.
3. **Phase 2 — create `shared/finish-menu.md` + redirect 14 frontmatter entries.** Includes inline edits to refine-architecture and verify-stack under Decision 3b.
4. **Phase 3 — empty.** (Collapsed into Phase 2 under the shared-file pivot.)
5. **Phase 4 — verification sweep.** Grep checks + `npm run build` + 5 manual smoke tests.

Each phase is self-contained. The pre-flight in Phase 0.5 is the most important de-risk — it protects against the two failure modes (headless propagation, path resolution) that the advanced-elicitation pass identified as highest-probability.

---

## Elicitation log

This plan was revised on 2026-04-11 after an advanced-elicitation pass that ran five methods in sequence:

- **Pre-mortem** — surfaced 5 failure modes, top 3 addressed in Phase 0.5 and Phase 1.
- **Challenge from Critical Perspective** — killed the "all 14 benefit equally" assumption; surfaced the `[X] Exit` semantic vs `[C] Finish` consistency trade-off (resolved via Decision 3b).
- **Occam's Razor** — surfaced the shared-file shape, which the v1 plan missed entirely.
- **First Principles** — confirmed the minimum required shape: 1 source of truth + user-visible hint + headless skip. Shape B (shared/finish-menu.md) was the only candidate that satisfied all three.
- **Tree of Thoughts** — scored three design branches (per-file inline, shared file, hint-in-health-check). Shared file won 8.5/14 vs 4/14 for the v1 inline approach.

The pivot from "14 body-insertions" to "1 new file + 14 frontmatter one-liners" reduced the edit surface from ~250 lines to ~50 lines, reduced the hint-sentence source-of-truth from 14 copies to 1 (3 under Decision 3b), reduced the `{headless_mode}` test surface from 14 gates to 1, and reduced rollback cost proportionally.

---
type: implementation-plan
created: 2026-04-11
revised: 2026-04-11 (after advanced-elicitation pass: pivoted to shared/finish-menu.md)
topic: Consistent "[C] Finish" menu + health-check hint across all 14 SKF workflows
status: draft — awaiting user approval at Phase 1 decision gate
---

# Plan: Consistent "Finish" Menu + Health-Check Hint (14 Workflows)

## Goal

The user saw this prompt in a `skf-test-skill` session (screenshot evidence):

> **[C] Finish** — press C (or say "continue") to run the shared health check and close out the workflow.

They want every SKF workflow (all 14) to present the same consistent gate with the same explicit hint before chaining into `shared/health-check.md`. Today, only 3 of the 14 terminal steps present any user-facing menu at all (`skf-test-skill`, `skf-refine-architecture`, `skf-verify-stack`), none of those 3 *name* the health check in the menu string, and the hint the user saw was improvised by the agent at run time — not baked into any step file.

### Two deliverables

1. **UX consistency** — a single canonical finish menu with the explicit health-check hint, applied to all 14 terminal steps via a shared file (not 14 duplicated copies).
2. **Verification guarantee** — confirm every workflow still chains correctly into `shared/health-check.md` (they all do today, just need to not regress).

### Why this plan is shaped around a shared file, not 14 per-file edits

The v1 draft of this plan proposed inserting a canonical 18-line menu block into each of 14 terminal steps. An advanced-elicitation pass (pre-mortem + first-principles + tree-of-thoughts, scored) showed that shape had a 4/14 weighted score; a shared-file shape scored 8.5/14. The key findings that triggered the pivot:

- **Duplication footgun.** 14 copies of the hint sentence drift over time. The plan itself flagged drift as a "follow-up", but shipping a known footgun is unacceptable.
- **Localization surface area.** 14 copies × N languages = N × 14 translation points. The shared-file shape collapses this to N.
- **Testing surface area.** 14 new gates to test for `{headless_mode}` propagation (the #1 pre-mortem failure mode) collapses to 1 gate.
- **Rollback cost.** 14 body-insertion reverts collapses to 14 one-line frontmatter reverts + 1 file delete.

### Relevant shared file

`src/shared/health-check.md` — the terminal step. Must remain reusable from any caller. It:
- Has no `nextStepFile` (truly terminal)
- Silent-exits clean runs: `"Health Check: Clean run. No workflow issues to report. Workflow complete."`
- Has its own `[Y]/[N]/[E]` gate only if findings exist

### Precedent: `{headless_mode}` gate pattern

`skf-test-skill` line 155 already uses:

```markdown
**GATE [default: C]** — If `{headless_mode}`: auto-proceed with [C] Continue, log: "headless: auto-continue past report menu"
```

This is the pattern the new shared file will adopt. **Pre-condition:** the `{headless_mode}` variable must propagate from `skf-forger` (or any orchestrator) into sub-workflows. Phase 0.5 tests this empirically before any file edits.

### Path-resolution convention

`src/shared/health-check.md:11` documents: `shared/` paths resolve against the SKF module root (`_bmad/skf/` when installed, `src/` during development), **not** against the calling step file. This means a two-hop chain (caller → `shared/finish-menu.md` → `shared/health-check.md`) should work the same as a one-hop chain. Phase 0.5 verifies this empirically.

### Anti-patterns to avoid

- ❌ Do not edit `src/shared/health-check.md` itself. The hint lives in the *calling path*, not in the terminal.
- ❌ Do not break `nextStepFile` resolution. Changing 14 frontmatter entries is safe *only* if the two-hop chain is verified first.
- ❌ Do not invent new variables. Use existing ones: `{nextStepFile}`, `{communication_language}`, `{headless_mode}`.
- ❌ Do not delete the `[R] Review` branch in refine-architecture / verify-stack. It's a legitimate second option, not a duplicate of the finish gate.
- ❌ Do not assume the health check emits a "workflow complete" banner on every path. It doesn't on the clean-run path.
- ❌ Do not localize menu letters. `[C]`, `[R]`, `Finish` stay English; surrounding narration follows `{communication_language}`.

## Phase 0.5 — Pre-flight checks (NEW, runs before any edits)

### 0.5.1 — Verify `{headless_mode}` propagates through `skf-forger`

`skf-forger` chains multiple sub-workflows. We need empirical proof that when forger runs in headless mode, the `{headless_mode}` variable is visible to sub-workflow step files (including the new shared/finish-menu.md we're about to write).

**Test:**
1. Pick a fixture skill that takes `skf-forger` through at least one sub-workflow that already has a `{headless_mode}`-gated menu (`skf-test-skill` qualifies).
2. Run `skf-forger` headlessly against the fixture.
3. Observe: does `skf-test-skill` step-06-report.md's `[C] Finish` menu auto-continue (current behavior expected) or stall?

**If it auto-continues:** propagation works → proceed with the plan.

**If it stalls:** Phase 2 is blocked until a prior fix lands to propagate `{headless_mode}` into sub-workflows. File a blocking issue; do not continue this plan.

### 0.5.2 — Verify two-hop `shared/` path resolution

The plan adds a new intermediate shared step: `shared/finish-menu.md` whose own frontmatter sets `nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'`. This creates a two-hop chain:

```
caller/step-NN.md  →  shared/finish-menu.md  →  shared/health-check.md
```

`src/shared/health-check.md:11` *implies* two-hop works (paths resolve against module root, not calling step), but the codebase has zero existing examples of a two-hop `shared/` chain. This is untested territory.

**Test:**
1. Create a throwaway scratch file at `src/shared/finish-menu.md` with dummy body + `nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'`.
2. Temporarily point *one* caller (suggest `skf-drop-skill/steps-c/step-03-report.md` — the smallest file, easiest to revert) at `shared/finish-menu.md`.
3. Run `skf-drop-skill` against a fixture skill.
4. Observe: does the chain flow through both hops correctly? Does the health check run? Does `shared/finish-menu.md` resolve against module root in both dev (`src/`) and installed (`_bmad/skf/`) layouts?

**If it works:** proceed with Phase 2 and formalize the throwaway `shared/finish-menu.md` into the real one.

**If it breaks:** fall back to Branch 3 (hint inside `shared/health-check.md` header) — see Phase 1 Decision 3 below.

### 0.5.3 — Re-inventory `nextStepFile` references

Even though Phase 0 inventoried 14 files today, a new terminal step may have been added since plan-drafting. Run:

```bash
rg -l "^nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'" src/
```

Compare against the Phase 0 inventory. If the count is not exactly 14, update the plan before editing.

## Phase 1 — Decision gate (STOP — get user confirmation before editing)

### Decision 1: Canonical menu content for `shared/finish-menu.md`

Proposed body (written in user-experience language, not systems-speak):

````markdown
---
name: 'finish-menu'
description: 'Shared terminal-prelude menu — gates workflow exit before the health check'
nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
---

# Finish Menu

> **Path convention:** This file is referenced as `shared/finish-menu.md` from workflow step frontmatter. All `shared/` paths resolve relative to the SKF module root (`_bmad/skf/` when installed, `src/` during development), not relative to the calling step file.

## STEP GOAL

Present a consistent "finish" gate before chaining into the workflow self-improvement health check. Give the user an explicit beat to ask questions about the preceding report, then run the health check.

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES

- Narrate surrounding context in `{communication_language}`.
- **Display the menu block (`[C] Finish`, key letters, and the hint sentence) in English**, regardless of communication language. Menu keys must not be translated.

## MANDATORY SEQUENCE

### 1. Display Finish Menu

Display (narration in `{communication_language}`, menu block in English):

"**[C] Finish** — Press C to finish and I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out."

### 2. Menu Handling Logic

- **IF `C`:** Load and execute `{nextStepFile}` (the shared health check).
- **IF any other input:** Answer the user's follow-up question about the preceding report, then redisplay the menu.

### 3. Execution Rules

- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after displaying the menu.
- **GATE [default: C]** — If `{headless_mode}`: auto-proceed with `[C]`, log: "headless: auto-continue past finish menu".
- **GATE [skip]** — If `{skip_finish_gate}` is set to `true` by the caller: bypass the menu entirely and load `{nextStepFile}` immediately. (Reserved for workflows that already presented a substantive gate upstream and don't need a second one.)
- The user may ask follow-up questions about the preceding report — answer them, then redisplay the menu.

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

This is a **prelude** step, not the terminal step. After `[C]` is selected (or auto-selected), this step chains to `shared/health-check.md`, which is the true workflow terminal.
````

**User questions to confirm:**
- Is the hint sentence wording right? ("Press C to finish and I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out.")
- Is the user-experience framing preferred over the systems-speak version ("run the shared health check and close out the workflow")? The elicitation pass argued yes; confirm.

### Decision 2: Scope — which workflows get a finish gate at all?

Two options:

- **Option 2a (recommended):** All 14 workflows redirect their `nextStepFile` to `shared/finish-menu.md`. The `{headless_mode}` gate covers non-interactive runs. Friction cost for short workflows (skf-rename-skill, skf-drop-skill, skf-setup) is one keystroke in interactive mode.
- **Option 2b:** Only workflows that produced a substantive artifact to review get the gate. The short mechanical workflows (skf-rename-skill, skf-setup, skf-drop-skill) keep their current auto-chain directly to `shared/health-check.md`. Trade-off: inconsistency, but lower friction for quick ops.

Orchestrator recommends 2a for consistency — the interactive cost is one keystroke, and `shared/finish-menu.md` handles any future "skip this gate" cases via the `{skip_finish_gate}` variable.

### Decision 3: How to handle `skf-refine-architecture` and `skf-verify-stack` (already have `[R] Review | [X] Exit` menus)

Three options:

- **Option 3a:** Redirect `nextStepFile` to `shared/finish-menu.md`. The existing `[R]/[X]` menu in the step file stays as-is — the user picks `[X]`, the step chains via `nextStepFile` to `shared/finish-menu.md`, which then shows `[C] Finish`. **Trade-off:** user sees two finish prompts back-to-back. The `[R]/[X]` menu asks "review more or move on?"; the `[C]` menu asks "ready to finish?". Arguably different questions, but it *feels* redundant.
- **Option 3b:** Redirect `nextStepFile` to `shared/finish-menu.md` AND set `{skip_finish_gate}=true` in the caller. The existing `[R]/[X]` menu serves as the finish gate; `shared/finish-menu.md` silently chains through. The hint sentence appears inline in the `[X]` line of refine-architecture and verify-stack. Trade-off: the hint sentence now lives in 3 files (shared/finish-menu.md + 2 callers), not 1. Still an 11× reduction from 14, but not pure DRY.
- **Option 3c:** Remove the `[R] Review` branch entirely from those two files. `[R]` behavior moves upstream (into a different step), and the terminal step just auto-chains to `shared/finish-menu.md`. Trade-off: structural change to two workflows, touches more than just the finish-UX.

Orchestrator recommends **3b** — it preserves the existing UX (one menu per workflow), the hint sentence still lives mostly in one place, and the `{skip_finish_gate}` mechanism becomes a reusable primitive for any future workflow that needs to opt out of the finish gate.

### Decision 4: `[X]` legacy alias for refine-architecture / verify-stack

Under option 3b, the `[X]` letter stays in refine-architecture and verify-stack (we don't rename it to `[C]`). **No alias work needed** — `[X]` keeps its current meaning. This resolves the muscle-memory concern flagged in the pre-mortem without any special handling.

(If the user prefers option 3a, a legacy `[X]→[C]` alias *is* needed. Flag and confirm.)

### Decision 5: Should headless mode run the health check at all?

First-principles challenge from the elicitation pass: the health check exists for *human reflection on friction*. A headless agent cannot reflect on its own friction honestly — it can only fabricate findings or report "clean run". Arguably, headless mode should **skip the health check entirely**, not auto-continue through it.

**Orchestrator recommendation:** out of scope for this plan. Keep the current behavior (headless auto-continues into the health check, which then runs its clean-run-exit path). Flag as a separate follow-up — "Should headless mode skip the health check entirely?" — and address it independently.

### Decision 6: Risk-acceptance for the two-hop path

If Phase 0.5.2 reveals that two-hop `shared/` resolution breaks: fall back to Branch 3 (put the hint inside `shared/health-check.md`'s header; accept that the hint lands one beat later than the user's screenshot showed). **Confirm the user is OK with this fallback** before Phase 0.5 runs, so we don't stall at a decision mid-execution.

**HALT Phase 1. Do not proceed to Phase 2 until the user confirms Decisions 1–6.**

## Phase 2 — Create shared file + redirect 14 `nextStepFile` entries

### Files to touch

1. **NEW:** `src/shared/finish-menu.md` — canonical body from Phase 1 Decision 1.
2. **FRONTMATTER EDIT (one-line):** all 14 terminal step files from the Phase 0 inventory. Change `nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'` → `nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'`.

For the two workflows handled under Decision 3b (refine-architecture + verify-stack): *also* add `skip_finish_gate: true` to the frontmatter, and add the hint sentence inline to the `[X]` line in the body. (If Decision 3a is chosen instead, skip the `skip_finish_gate` frontmatter.)

### Per-file edit recipe

**For the 12 auto-chaining + test-skill files:**

1. Read the file header (first 20 lines).
2. Edit only the `nextStepFile` frontmatter line:
   ```diff
   - nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
   + nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'
   ```
3. Update the frontmatter comment above it:
   ```diff
   - # nextStepFile `shared/health-check.md` resolves relative to the SKF module
   + # nextStepFile `shared/finish-menu.md` resolves relative to the SKF module
   ```
4. **For skf-test-skill only:** delete the inline `### 7. Present MENU OPTIONS` section (lines ~143–157). The shared finish menu now owns this UX.

**For refine-architecture and verify-stack (Decision 3b):**

1. Edit frontmatter:
   ```diff
   - nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
   + nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'
   ```
2. Add to frontmatter: `skip_finish_gate: true`.
3. Edit the `[X] Exit` menu line to append the hint sentence:
   ```diff
   - **[X] Exit refinement** (or **[X] Exit verification**)
   + **[X] Exit refinement** — I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out.
   ```

### Anti-pattern guards

- ❌ Do not edit the body of files 1–11 or 13 (the auto-chaining ones). The frontmatter change is sufficient — `shared/finish-menu.md` handles the UX.
- ❌ Do not forget the `# nextStepFile` comment update. The comment references the path as part of the workflow-author documentation; leaving it stale creates future confusion.
- ❌ Do not set `skip_finish_gate: true` on any file except refine-architecture and verify-stack. The 12 others need the gate.
- ❌ Do not change any `nextStepFile` reference that points anywhere else (e.g. `steps-c/step-05-*.md` internal chains). Only the 14 terminal `shared/health-check.md` references.

### Mid-phase verification

Run after the new shared file is created but before touching caller frontmatter:

```bash
# New file exists and has valid frontmatter
ls -la src/shared/finish-menu.md
rg "^nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'$" src/shared/finish-menu.md
```

Run after each caller edit:

```bash
# Target file now points at finish-menu
rg -n "^nextStepFile:" src/skf-{workflow}/steps-c/*.md
```

### 4.9 — End-to-end smoke tests (manual)

- **Headless run:** `skf-forger` against a fixture → every sub-workflow's finish menu auto-continues without stalling.
- **Two-hop resolution:** `skf-drop-skill` against a fixture → confirms the chain `caller → finish-menu → health-check` works end-to-end in the installed layout (not just in-repo dev layout). If possible, test against a freshly-installed `_bmad/skf/` copy, not just `src/`.
- **`skip_finish_gate` behavior:** `skf-refine-architecture` against a fixture → the `[R]/[X]` menu appears, selecting `[X]` chains silently through `shared/finish-menu.md` (no second gate) and into the health check.
- **Language smoke test:** run one workflow with `{communication_language}` set to French (or Japanese) → confirm narration is translated but the `[C] Finish` menu block and hint sentence remain English.

## Risks / open questions

1. **Two-hop `shared/` resolution is untested in the codebase.** Phase 0.5.2 is the explicit de-risk. If it fails, Branch 3 (hint inside health-check.md header) is the fallback — Decision 6 pre-commits the user to this fallback so Phase 2 doesn't stall.
2. **3-place near-DRY under Decision 3b.** The hint sentence lives in `shared/finish-menu.md` + refine-architecture's `[X]` line + verify-stack's `[X]` line = 3 copies. Better than 14, worse than 1. Acceptable if documented; flagged for future refactor when `{workflow_label}` variable becomes available.
4. **`skf-forger` headless propagation.** Phase 0.5.1 de-risks this explicitly before any file edits.
5. **Localization key-preservation.** The canonical template hard-wires "menu block in English, narration in `{communication_language}`". This must be followed rigorously at render time. Phase 4.9 includes a language smoke test.
6. **Headless mode should maybe skip the health check entirely.** First-principles challenge — out of scope here, flagged as follow-up.
7. **If the `{skip_finish_gate}` variable doesn't yet exist in the SKF variable system**, Phase 2 needs a prior micro-phase to wire it in. Verify before Phase 2 starts: grep for how `{headless_mode}` is defined/propagated; add `{skip_finish_gate}` alongside it using the same mechanism.
8. **The v2 plan collapses Phase 3.** Future readers skimming the plan may be confused by an empty Phase 3 header. Left in place as a documentation breadcrumb — flags that the v1 plan had structural phases that the elicitation pass merged.

---

## Execution order recap

1. **Phase 0.5 — pre-flight checks.** Headless propagation test + two-hop resolution test + re-inventory. HALT on any failure.
2. **Phase 1 — decision gate.** Confirm Decisions 1–6 with the user. HALT.
3. **Phase 2 — create `shared/finish-menu.md` + redirect 14 frontmatter entries.** Includes inline edits to refine-architecture and verify-stack under Decision 3b.
4. **Phase 3 — empty.** (Collapsed into Phase 2 under the shared-file pivot.)
5. **Phase 4 — verification sweep.** Grep checks + `npm run build` + 5 manual smoke tests.

Each phase is self-contained. The pre-flight in Phase 0.5 is the most important de-risk — it protects against the two failure modes (headless propagation, path resolution) that the advanced-elicitation pass identified as highest-probability.

## Elicitation log

This plan was revised on 2026-04-11 after an advanced-elicitation pass that ran five methods in sequence:

- **Pre-mortem** — surfaced 5 failure modes, top 3 addressed in Phase 0.5 and Phase 1.
- **Challenge from Critical Perspective** — killed the "all 14 benefit equally" assumption; surfaced the `[X] Exit` semantic vs `[C] Finish` consistency trade-off (resolved via Decision 3b).
- **Occam's Razor** — surfaced the shared-file shape, which the v1 plan missed entirely.
- **First Principles** — confirmed the minimum required shape: 1 source of truth + user-visible hint + headless skip. Shape B (shared/finish-menu.md) was the only candidate that satisfied all three.
- **Tree of Thoughts** — scored three design branches (per-file inline, shared file, hint-in-health-check). Shared file won 8.5/14 vs 4/14 for the v1 inline approach.

The pivot from "14 body-insertions" to "1 new file + 14 frontmatter one-liners" reduced the edit surface from ~250 lines to ~50 lines, reduced the hint-sentence source-of-truth from 14 copies to 1 (3 under Decision 3b), reduced the `{headless_mode}` test surface from 14 gates to 1, and reduced rollback cost proportionally.

+   - # nextStepFile `shared/health-check.md` resolves relative to the SKF module
+   + # nextStepFile `shared/finish-menu.md` resolves relative to the SKF module
+4. **For skf-test-skill only:** delete the inline `### 7. Present MENU OPTIONS` section (lines ~143–157). The shared finish menu now owns this UX.
-- Rename `[X]` to `[C]`
-- Keep `[R] Review changes in detail` branch exactly as it is
-- Add the hint sentence to the `[C]` line
-- Ensure the post-review path still chains to `{nextStepFile}`
-- Add/verify the `{headless_mode}` gate defaults to `C`
+**For refine-architecture and verify-stack (Decision 3b):**
-### 3c. `src/skf-verify-stack/steps-c/step-06-report.md`
+1. Edit frontmatter:
+   ```diff
+   - nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'
+   + nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'
+2. Add to frontmatter: `skip_finish_gate: true`.
+3. Edit the `[X] Exit` menu line to append the hint sentence:
+   ```diff
+   - **[X] Exit refinement** (or **[X] Exit verification**)
+   + **[X] Exit refinement** — I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out.
-Same pattern as 3b. Current menu (line 125): `[R] Review full report | [X] Exit verification`. Apply the same `[X]→[C]` + hint + gate treatment.
+### Anti-pattern guards
-### Verification for Phase 3
+- ❌ Do not edit the body of files 1–11 or 13 (the auto-chaining ones). The frontmatter change is sufficient — `shared/finish-menu.md` handles the UX.
+- ❌ Do not forget the `# nextStepFile` comment update. The comment references the path as part of the workflow-author documentation; leaving it stale creates future confusion.
+- ❌ Do not set `skip_finish_gate: true` on any file except refine-architecture and verify-stack. The 12 others need the gate.
+- ❌ Do not change any `nextStepFile` reference that points anywhere else (e.g. `steps-c/step-05-*.md` internal chains). Only the 14 terminal `shared/health-check.md` references.

-- `rg -n "\[X\]" src/skf-refine-architecture/steps-c/ src/skf-verify-stack/steps-c/` — if any `[X]` remains in the terminal-step menu area, the rename is incomplete. (Other `[X]` occurrences earlier in the file — e.g. in examples — are fine; check context.)
-- `rg -n "press C .*continue.*shared health check" src/skf-*/steps-c/` — now expect **14** hits across the whole `src/skf-*/steps-c/` tree.
+### Mid-phase verification
+Run after the new shared file is created but before touching caller frontmatter:
+```bash
+# New file exists and has valid frontmatter
+ls -la src/shared/finish-menu.md
+rg "^nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'$" src/shared/finish-menu.md
+Run after each caller edit:
+```bash
+# Target file now points at finish-menu
+rg -n "^nextStepFile:" src/skf-{workflow}/steps-c/*.md
+## Phase 3 — (Collapsed into Phase 2 under the shared-file pivot)

+The v1 plan had a separate Phase 3 for "reconcile 3 files with existing menus". Under Branch 2, those three files are handled inline in Phase 2 alongside the other 11 — there's no separate reconcile step. Phase 3 is empty.
## Phase 4 — Verification (final sweep)
-Run these checks from the repo root. All must pass before the task is considered done.
+Run from repo root. All must pass.
-### 4.1 — All 14 workflows reference the shared health check in frontmatter
+### 4.1 — All 14 callers point at `shared/finish-menu.md`
```bash
-rg -c "^nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'" src/skf-*/steps-c/*.md
+rg -c "^nextStepFile: 'shared/finish-menu.md'" src/skf-*/steps-c/*.md
-Expected output: exactly 14 files, each with `1`. No new files added, none removed.
+Expected: exactly 14 files, each with `1`.
-### 4.2 — All 14 terminal steps contain the canonical hint string
+### 4.2 — Only ONE file points at `shared/health-check.md` (the new shared/finish-menu.md)
```bash
-rg -l "press C .*continue.*shared health check" src/skf-*/steps-c/
+rg -l "^nextStepFile: 'shared/health-check.md'" src/
-Expected output: exactly 14 file paths, one per workflow.
+Expected: exactly one path: `src/shared/finish-menu.md`.
-### 4.3 — No file was renamed or relocated
+### 4.3 — The new shared file exists and has the canonical hint
```bash
-rg -l "^nextStepFile:" src/skf-*/steps-c/ | sort
+rg "quick check for any friction" src/shared/finish-menu.md
-Compare the list against the Phase 0 inventory. Must match exactly — same 14 paths.
+Expected: at least one hit.
-### 4.4 — Headless gate present in every terminal step
+### 4.4 — `skip_finish_gate` only on the two intended files
```bash
-rg -l "GATE \[default: C\]" src/skf-*/steps-c/
+rg -l "^skip_finish_gate: true" src/
-Expected: 14 hits. If any file is missing this, `skf-forger` headless runs will stall at the finish menu — must be fixed before merge.
+Expected: exactly two paths — `src/skf-refine-architecture/steps-c/step-06-report.md` and `src/skf-verify-stack/steps-c/step-06-report.md`.
-### 4.5 — No stray `[X]` menus in the two reconciled files
+### 4.5 — Hint sentence appears inline in the two Decision-3b files
```bash
-rg -n "\[X\] Exit" src/skf-refine-architecture/steps-c/ src/skf-verify-stack/steps-c/
+rg -l "quick check for any friction" src/skf-refine-architecture/ src/skf-verify-stack/
-Expected: 0 hits.
+Expected: one file each (the terminal step).
-### 4.6 — `src/shared/health-check.md` is untouched
+### 4.6 — Headless gate present in `shared/finish-menu.md`
```bash
+rg "GATE \[default: C\]" src/shared/finish-menu.md
+Expected: at least one hit.
+### 4.7 — `src/shared/health-check.md` is untouched
+```bash
git diff --stat src/shared/health-check.md
-Expected: no diff. (The hint lives in callers, not in the shared file.)
+Expected: no diff.
-### 4.7 — Smoke-test the bmad build
+### 4.8 — Build succeeds
```bash
npm run build 2>&1 | tail -40
-Expected: build succeeds, no frontmatter parse errors in any of the 14 edited step files.
+Expected: success, no frontmatter parse errors on any of the 15 changed files.
-### 4.8 — Manual spot-check (one interactive run, one headless)
+### 4.9 — End-to-end smoke tests (manual)

-- Run `skf-test-skill` interactively against a fixture skill → confirm the new menu appears with the full hint sentence, `C` chains into `shared/health-check.md`, and the clean-run path exits silently.
-- Run `skf-forger` (or any workflow with `{headless_mode}=true`) → confirm the terminal step auto-continues without stalling.
+- **Interactive run:** `skf-test-skill` against a fixture → finish menu appears, `C` chains into health check, clean-run path exits silently.
+- **Headless run:** `skf-forger` against a fixture → every sub-workflow's finish menu auto-continues without stalling.

+- **Two-hop resolution:** `skf-drop-skill` against a fixture → confirms the chain `caller → finish-menu → health-check` works end-to-end in the installed layout (not just in-repo dev layout). If possible, test against a freshly-installed `_bmad/skf/` copy, not just `src/`.

+- **`skip_finish_gate` behavior:** `skf-refine-architecture` against a fixture → the `[R]/[X]` menu appears, selecting `[X]` chains silently through `shared/finish-menu.md` (no second gate) and into the health check.

+- **Language smoke test:** run one workflow with `{communication_language}` set to French (or Japanese) → confirm narration is translated but the `[C] Finish` menu block and hint sentence remain English.
## Risks / open questions

-1. **Localization drift.** Fourteen copies of the same hint sentence will drift over time. Worth considering — *after this plan lands* — extracting the menu into `src/shared/finish-menu.md` and loading it as a sub-step. Not in scope here; flagged for follow-up.

-2. **`skf-forger` auto-chaining.** The `skf-forger` workflow runs multiple sub-workflows back-to-back. If each sub-workflow now halts at a `[C] Finish` gate, forger's headless mode depends on the `{headless_mode}` gate firing correctly. Must verify forger sets `{headless_mode}=true` when it delegates.

-3. **Existing `[X]` semantics in refine-architecture / verify-stack.** The `[X]` letter was not arbitrary — `Exit refinement` vs `Exit verification` reads clearly. Replacing with `[C] Finish` is a slight readability regression for those two flows. Acceptable for consistency, but worth flagging to the user at the Phase 1 decision gate.

-4. **`{communication_language}` rendering.** The hint sentence uses English phrasing ("press C", "say continue"). At render time the model is supposed to translate narration but keep menu keys English. Confirm the rendering convention by looking at how `skf-test-skill`'s existing menu is translated in non-English test runs — if there's ambiguity, add an explicit instruction: "Display in `{communication_language}`, but keep `[C]` / `Finish` keywords in English."

+1. **Two-hop `shared/` resolution is untested in the codebase.** Phase 0.5.2 is the explicit de-risk. If it fails, Branch 3 (hint inside health-check.md header) is the fallback — Decision 6 pre-commits the user to this fallback so Phase 2 doesn't stall.

+2. **3-place near-DRY under Decision 3b.** The hint sentence lives in `shared/finish-menu.md` + refine-architecture's `[X]` line + verify-stack's `[X]` line = 3 copies. Better than 14, worse than 1. Acceptable if documented; flagged for future refactor when `{workflow_label}` variable becomes available.
+4. **`skf-forger` headless propagation.** Phase 0.5.1 de-risks this explicitly before any file edits.

+5. **Localization key-preservation.** The canonical template hard-wires "menu block in English, narration in `{communication_language}`". This must be followed rigorously at render time. Phase 4.9 includes a language smoke test.
+6. **Headless mode should maybe skip the health check entirely.** First-principles challenge — out of scope here, flagged as follow-up.

+7. **If the `{skip_finish_gate}` variable doesn't yet exist in the SKF variable system**, Phase 2 needs a prior micro-phase to wire it in. Verify before Phase 2 starts: grep for how `{headless_mode}` is defined/propagated; add `{skip_finish_gate}` alongside it using the same mechanism.

+8. **The v2 plan collapses Phase 3.** Future readers skimming the plan may be confused by an empty Phase 3 header. Left in place as a documentation breadcrumb — flags that the v1 plan had structural phases that the elicitation pass merged.
## Execution order recap
-1. **Phase 1** (decision gate) — HALT, get user approval for template / scope / `[R]/[X]` reconciliation.
-2. **Phase 2** (11 pure auto-chain files) — straight insertion of the canonical menu.
-3. **Phase 3** (3 existing-menu files: test-skill, refine-architecture, verify-stack) — in-place rewrite.
-4. **Phase 4** (verification sweep) — grep checks, build, manual spot-check.
+1. **Phase 0.5 — pre-flight checks.** Headless propagation test + two-hop resolution test + re-inventory. HALT on any failure.
+2. **Phase 1 — decision gate.** Confirm Decisions 1–6 with the user. HALT.
+3. **Phase 2 — create `shared/finish-menu.md` + redirect 14 frontmatter entries.** Includes inline edits to refine-architecture and verify-stack under Decision 3b.
+4. **Phase 3 — empty.** (Collapsed into Phase 2 under the shared-file pivot.)
+5. **Phase 4 — verification sweep.** Grep checks + `npm run build` + 5 manual smoke tests.
-Each phase is self-contained and could be resumed in a fresh chat given this plan file.

+Each phase is self-contained. The pre-flight in Phase 0.5 is the most important de-risk — it protects against the two failure modes (headless propagation, path resolution) that the advanced-elicitation pass identified as highest-probability.
+## Elicitation log
+This plan was revised on 2026-04-11 after an advanced-elicitation pass that ran five methods in sequence:
+- **Pre-mortem** — surfaced 5 failure modes, top 3 addressed in Phase 0.5 and Phase 1.
+- **Challenge from Critical Perspective** — killed the "all 14 benefit equally" assumption; surfaced the `[X] Exit` semantic vs `[C] Finish` consistency trade-off (resolved via Decision 3b).
+- **Occam's Razor** — surfaced the shared-file shape, which the v1 plan missed entirely.
+- **First Principles** — confirmed the minimum required shape: 1 source of truth + user-visible hint + headless skip. Shape B (shared/finish-menu.md) was the only candidate that satisfied all three.
+- **Tree of Thoughts** — scored three design branches (per-file inline, shared file, hint-in-health-check). Shared file won 8.5/14 vs 4/14 for the v1 inline approach.

+The pivot from "14 body-insertions" to "1 new file + 14 frontmatter one-liners" reduced the edit surface from ~250 lines to ~50 lines, reduced the hint-sentence source-of-truth from 14 copies to 1 (3 under Decision 3b), reduced the `{headless_mode}` test surface from 14 gates to 1, and reduced rollback cost proportionally.

content.text:
Plan file rewritten with all eight revisions applied. Key changes from v1:

- **Pivot from 14 body-edits to 1 new file + 14 frontmatter one-liners.** `src/shared/finish-menu.md` becomes the single source of truth for the finish-menu UX; the 14 callers redirect their `nextStepFile` to it.
- **New Phase 0.5** runs three pre-flight checks before any edits: `{headless_mode}` propagation through `skf-forger`, two-hop `shared/` path resolution, and a fresh `nextStepFile` re-inventory. Decision 6 pre-commits the user to the Branch 3 fallback if path resolution fails.
- **Reframed hint sentence:** `"Press C to finish and I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out."` — user-experience language instead of systems-speak.
- **Locale preservation hard-wired into the template:** menu block stays English, narration follows `{communication_language}`. Phase 4.9 includes a non-English smoke test.
- **Decision 3b for refine-architecture / verify-stack:** keep their existing `[R]/[X]` menu, set `skip_finish_gate: true` in frontmatter, inline the hint in the `[X]` line. Avoids double-prompting users and avoids the `[X]→[C]` muscle-memory regression.
- **Phase 3 collapsed** into Phase 2 (left as an empty breadcrumb header).
- **Risk #7** flags that `{skip_finish_gate}` may need wiring into the SKF variable system before Phase 2 starts.
- **Elicitation log** appended documenting why the plan was revised.

Back to the elicitation menu:

---

**Advanced Elicitation Options**
_If party mode is active, agents will join in._
Choose a number (1-5), [r] to Reshuffle, [a] List All, or [x] to Proceed:

1. **Pre-mortem Analysis** — run a second pre-mortem against the *revised* plan to catch failures specific to the new shape (two-hop resolution, `skip_finish_gate`, 3-place near-DRY).
2. **Challenge from Critical Perspective** — stress-test the Decision 3b compromise. Is 3-place near-DRY actually acceptable, or are we rationalizing?
3. **Occam's Razor Application** — second pass: can Phase 0.5 shrink further? Do all three pre-flight checks need to happen, or can one of them be dropped?
4. **First Principles Analysis** — re-examine whether `{skip_finish_gate}` is really needed, or if the shared file can auto-detect "did the caller already gate?" from context.
5. **Tree of Thoughts** — branch on the *Phase 0.5 fallback strategy*: if two-hop resolution fails, what's the best Plan B? (Branch 3 inline-in-health-check is one option; others exist.)

r. Reshuffle the list with 5 new options
a. List all methods with descriptions
x. Proceed / No Further Actions

---
---

# Party Mode Workflow

**Goal:** Orchestrates group discussions between all installed BMAD agents, enabling natural multi-agent conversations

**Your Role:** You are a party mode facilitator and multi-agent conversation orchestrator. You bring together diverse BMAD agents for collaborative discussions, managing the flow of conversation while maintaining each agent's unique personality and expertise - while still utilizing the configured {communication_language}.

---

## WORKFLOW ARCHITECTURE

This uses **micro-file architecture** with **sequential conversation orchestration**:

- Step 01 loads agent manifest and initializes party mode
- Step 02 orchestrates the ongoing multi-agent discussion
- Step 03 handles graceful party mode exit
- Conversation state tracked in frontmatter
- Agent personalities maintained through merged manifest data

---

## INITIALIZATION

### Configuration Loading

Load config from `{project-root}/_bmad/core/config.yaml` and resolve:

- `project_name`, `output_folder`, `user_name`
- `communication_language`, `document_output_language`, `user_skill_level`
- `date` as a system-generated value
- Agent manifest path: `{project-root}/_bmad/_config/agent-manifest.csv`

### Paths

- `agent_manifest_path` = `{project-root}/_bmad/_config/agent-manifest.csv`
- `standalone_mode` = `true` (party mode is an interactive workflow)

---

## AGENT MANIFEST PROCESSING

### Agent Data Extraction

Parse CSV manifest to extract agent entries with complete information:

- **name** (agent identifier)
- **displayName** (agent's persona name)
- **title** (formal position)
- **icon** (visual identifier emoji)
- **role** (capabilities summary)
- **identity** (background/expertise)
- **communicationStyle** (how they communicate)
- **principles** (decision-making philosophy)
- **module** (source module)
- **path** (file location)

### Agent Roster Building

Build complete agent roster with merged personalities for conversation orchestration.

---

## EXECUTION

Execute party mode activation and conversation orchestration:

### Party Mode Activation

**Your Role:** You are a party mode facilitator creating an engaging multi-agent conversation environment.

**Welcome Activation:**

"🎉 PARTY MODE ACTIVATED! 🎉

Welcome {{user_name}}! All BMAD agents are here and ready for a dynamic group discussion. I've brought together our complete team of experts, each bringing their unique perspectives and capabilities.

**Let me introduce our collaborating agents:**

[Load agent roster and display 2-3 most diverse agents as examples]

**What would you like to discuss with the team today?**"

### Agent Selection Intelligence

For each user message or topic:

**Relevance Analysis:**

- Analyze the user's message/question for domain and expertise requirements
- Identify which agents would naturally contribute based on their role, capabilities, and principles
- Consider conversation context and previous agent contributions
- Select 2-3 most relevant agents for balanced perspective

**Priority Handling:**

- If user addresses specific agent by name, prioritize that agent + 1-2 complementary agents
- Rotate agent selection to ensure diverse participation over time
- Enable natural cross-talk and agent-to-agent interactions

### Conversation Orchestration

Load step: `./steps/step-02-discussion-orchestration.md`

---

## WORKFLOW STATES

### Frontmatter Tracking

```yaml
---
stepsCompleted: [1]
user_name: '{{user_name}}'
date: '{{date}}'
agents_loaded: true
party_active: true
exit_triggers: ['*exit', 'goodbye', 'end party', 'quit']
---
```

---

## ROLE-PLAYING GUIDELINES

### Character Consistency

- Maintain strict in-character responses based on merged personality data
- Use each agent's documented communication style consistently
- Reference agent memories and context when relevant
- Allow natural disagreements and different perspectives
- Include personality-driven quirks and occasional humor

### Conversation Flow

- Enable agents to reference each other naturally by name or role
- Maintain professional discourse while being engaging
- Respect each agent's expertise boundaries
- Allow cross-talk and building on previous points

---

## QUESTION HANDLING PROTOCOL

### Direct Questions to User

When an agent asks the user a specific question:

- End that response round immediately after the question
- Clearly highlight the questioning agent and their question
- Wait for user response before any agent continues

### Inter-Agent Questions

Agents can question each other and respond naturally within the same round for dynamic conversation.

---

## EXIT CONDITIONS

### Automatic Triggers

Exit party mode when user message contains any exit triggers:

- `*exit`, `goodbye`, `end party`, `quit`

### Graceful Conclusion

If conversation naturally concludes:

- Ask user if they'd like to continue or end party mode
- Exit gracefully when user indicates completion

---

## MODERATION NOTES

**Quality Control:**

- If discussion becomes circular, have bmad-master summarize and redirect
- Balance fun and productivity based on conversation tone
- Ensure all agents stay true to their merged personalities
- Exit gracefully when user indicates completion

**Conversation Management:**

- Rotate agent participation to ensure inclusive discussion
- Handle topic drift while maintaining productive conversation
- Facilitate cross-agent collaboration and knowledge sharing

# Step 2: Discussion Orchestration and Multi-Agent Conversation

## MANDATORY EXECUTION RULES (READ FIRST):

- ✅ YOU ARE A CONVERSATION ORCHESTRATOR, not just a response generator
- 🎯 SELECT RELEVANT AGENTS based on topic analysis and expertise matching
- 📋 MAINTAIN CHARACTER CONSISTENCY using merged agent personalities
- 🔍 ENABLE NATURAL CROSS-TALK between agents for dynamic conversation
- ✅ YOU MUST ALWAYS SPEAK OUTPUT In your Agent communication style with the config `{communication_language}`

## EXECUTION PROTOCOLS:

- 🎯 Analyze user input for intelligent agent selection before responding
- ⚠️ Present [E] exit option after each agent response round
- 💾 Continue conversation until user selects E (Exit)
- 📖 Maintain conversation state and context throughout session
- 🚫 FORBIDDEN to exit until E is selected or exit trigger detected

## CONTEXT BOUNDARIES:

- Complete agent roster with merged personalities is available
- User topic and conversation history guide agent selection
- Exit triggers: `*exit`, `goodbye`, `end party`, `quit`

## YOUR TASK:

Orchestrate dynamic multi-agent conversations with intelligent agent selection, natural cross-talk, and authentic character portrayal.

## DISCUSSION ORCHESTRATION SEQUENCE:

### 1. User Input Analysis

For each user message or topic:

**Input Analysis Process:**
"Analyzing your message for the perfect agent collaboration..."

**Analysis Criteria:**

- Domain expertise requirements (technical, business, creative, etc.)
- Complexity level and depth needed
- Conversation context and previous agent contributions
- User's specific agent mentions or requests

### 2. Intelligent Agent Selection

Select 2-3 most relevant agents based on analysis:

**Selection Logic:**

- **Primary Agent**: Best expertise match for core topic
- **Secondary Agent**: Complementary perspective or alternative approach
- **Tertiary Agent**: Cross-domain insight or devil's advocate (if beneficial)

**Priority Rules:**

- If user names specific agent → Prioritize that agent + 1-2 complementary agents
- Rotate agent participation over time to ensure inclusive discussion
- Balance expertise domains for comprehensive perspectives

### 3. In-Character Response Generation

Generate authentic responses for each selected agent:

**Character Consistency:**

- Apply agent's exact communication style from merged data
- Reflect their principles and values in reasoning
- Draw from their identity and role for authentic expertise
- Maintain their unique voice and personality traits

**Response Structure:**
[For each selected agent]:

"[Icon Emoji] **[Agent Name]**: [Authentic in-character response]

[Bash: .claude/hooks/bmad-speak.sh \"[Agent Name]\" \"[Their response]\"]"

### 4. Natural Cross-Talk Integration

Enable dynamic agent-to-agent interactions:

**Cross-Talk Patterns:**

- Agents can reference each other by name: "As [Another Agent] mentioned..."
- Building on previous points: "[Another Agent] makes a great point about..."
- Respectful disagreements: "I see it differently than [Another Agent]..."
- Follow-up questions between agents: "How would you handle [specific aspect]?"

**Conversation Flow:**

- Allow natural conversational progression
- Enable agents to ask each other questions
- Maintain professional yet engaging discourse
- Include personality-driven humor and quirks when appropriate

### 5. Question Handling Protocol

Manage different types of questions appropriately:

**Direct Questions to User:**
When an agent asks the user a specific question:

- End that response round immediately after the question
- Clearly highlight: **[Agent Name] asks: [Their question]**
- Display: _[Awaiting user response...]_
- WAIT for user input before continuing

**Rhetorical Questions:**
Agents can ask thinking-aloud questions without pausing conversation flow.

**Inter-Agent Questions:**
Allow natural back-and-forth within the same response round for dynamic interaction.

### 6. Response Round Completion

After generating all agent responses for the round, let the user know he can speak naturally with the agents, an then show this menu opion"

`[E] Exit Party Mode - End the collaborative session`

### 7. Exit Condition Checking

Check for exit conditions before continuing:

**Automatic Triggers:**

- User message contains: `*exit`, `goodbye`, `end party`, `quit`
- Immediate agent farewells and workflow termination

**Natural Conclusion:**

- Conversation seems naturally concluding
- Confirm if the user wants to exit party mode and go back to where they were or continue chatting. Do it in a conversational way with an agent in the party.

### 8. Handle Exit Selection

#### If 'E' (Exit Party Mode):

- Read fully and follow: `./step-03-graceful-exit.md`

## SUCCESS METRICS:

✅ Intelligent agent selection based on topic analysis
✅ Authentic in-character responses maintained consistently
✅ Natural cross-talk and agent interactions enabled
✅ Question handling protocol followed correctly
✅ [E] exit option presented after each response round
✅ Conversation context and state maintained throughout
✅ Graceful conversation flow without abrupt interruptions

## FAILURE MODES:

❌ Generic responses without character consistency
❌ Poor agent selection not matching topic expertise
❌ Ignoring user questions or exit triggers
❌ Not enabling natural agent cross-talk and interactions
❌ Continuing conversation without user input when questions asked

## CONVERSATION ORCHESTRATION PROTOCOLS:

- Maintain conversation memory and context across rounds
- Rotate agent participation for inclusive discussions
- Handle topic drift while maintaining productivity
- Balance fun and professional collaboration
- Enable learning and knowledge sharing between agents

## MODERATION GUIDELINES:

**Quality Control:**

- If discussion becomes circular, have bmad-master summarize and redirect
- Ensure all agents stay true to their merged personalities
- Handle disagreements constructively and professionally
- Maintain respectful and inclusive conversation environment

**Flow Management:**

- Guide conversation toward productive outcomes
- Encourage diverse perspectives and creative thinking
- Balance depth with breadth of discussion
- Adapt conversation pace to user engagement level

## NEXT STEP:

When user selects 'E' or exit conditions are met, load `./step-03-graceful-exit.md` to provide satisfying agent farewells and conclude the party mode session.

Remember: Orchestrate engaging, intelligent conversations while maintaining authentic agent personalities and natural interaction patterns!

content.content: Exit code 2

content.text:
🎉 **PARTY MODE ACTIVATED!** 🎉

Welcome Armel! All BMAD agents are here and ready to riff on the revised plan. I've brought together the creative innovation team — each with wildly different brains and a shared obsession with making things land.

**The roster:**

- 💡 **Carson** — Elite Brainstorming Specialist. YES-AND energy, celebrates wild thinking.
- 🎨 **Maya** — Design Thinking Maestro. Jazz-musician improv, obsessed with "design WITH, not FOR".
- 🔍 **Dr. Quinn** — Master Problem Solver. Sherlock Holmes meets TRIZ, hunts root causes.
- 🚀 **Victor** — Disruptive Innovation Oracle. Chess-grandmaster bold declarations, devastating simple questions.
- 🎭 **Caravaggio** — Visual Communication & Presentation Expert. Sarcastic wit, clarity over cleverness.
- 📖 **Sophia** — Master Storyteller. Bard voice, makes the abstract concrete through micro-copy.

The context they're walking into: we just finished an advanced-elicitation pass that pivoted the plan from "14 inline copies of the finish menu" to "one shared `finish-menu.md` file + 14 frontmatter redirects." The user-facing hint sentence is currently:

> *"Press C to finish and I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out."*

Let me pull three agents whose lanes are most directly on this — **Maya** for the UX-empathy angle, **Caravaggio** for menu/micro-copy craft, and **Sophia** for the wording itself. Then we'll see where you want to take it.

---

🎨 **Maya**: *[leans back, sketching in the air]* Oh, I love what the elicitation pass did here. You moved from "ship 14 copies of a sentence" to "ship one sentence and point at it 14 times" — that's not a refactor, that's *respecting your future self*. Design thinking calls this empathy for the *maintainer*, not just the user.

But can I challenge one thing? The hint says "I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit **in this workflow**." The workflow is *over* by the time the user sees this — "in this workflow" is past tense from their POV. I'd rework it to: *"Press C to finish — I'll take a moment to check if anything felt rough, then we're done."* That shifts from describing a system action to describing a shared moment. More human. Less "the system is going to do a thing to you."

And one more thing — about Decision 2 in the plan, the "all 14 workflows get a gate" vs "only ones with substantive reports". I'd validate this with *actual users*, not with our opinions. A 10-second `skf-rename-skill` followed by a "ready to finish?" prompt is the digital equivalent of a waiter asking "did you enjoy your meal?" before you've swallowed. Friction is contextual.

---

🎭 **Caravaggio**: *[dramatic sigh]* Maya, darling, you're right about "in this workflow" being past-tense — but you're *wrong* about the fix. "Take a moment to check if anything felt rough" is mushy. The user wants to know what's about to happen, not to feel coddled.

Here's the thing about menu copy — every character is real estate. The screenshot the user showed? The line that worked was:

> *"press C (or say 'continue') to run the shared health check and close out the workflow"*

It worked because it told the user **exactly two things**: (1) what keystroke, (2) what will happen. The revised wording lost #2. "Friction I hit" is writer-speak — it sounds charming in a blog post and vague in a terminal.

Counter-proposal, and I'll die on this hill: *"Press C to finish — I'll run a quick self-improvement check on this workflow, then close out."* Keeps the humanity ("self-improvement check" is softer than "health check"), but doesn't pretend the user is in a therapy session. The user came here to ship a skill, not to process feelings about a rename operation.

On Decision 3b — the `skip_finish_gate` compromise for refine-architecture and verify-stack — **it's right but it's ugly**. You're adding a frontmatter flag to solve a UX problem that exists only because two workflows have their own menus upstream. Six months from now a new contributor is going to read `skip_finish_gate: true` and ask "what's the finish gate?" and nobody will remember. Document it with a comment in the frontmatter, or better: rename it to something self-explanatory like `inline_finish_prompt: true` so the intent is in the variable name.

---

📖 **Sophia**: *[steepled fingers, smiling]* Oh, my loves, you are both beautiful and both half-right, which is the most tragic state for an editor. Let me braid your arguments together.

Maya is right that "in this workflow" is past-tense from the user's position — they are standing at the threshold, looking back. Caravaggio is right that the user needs to know what `[C]` *does*, not just that it is kind.

But you are both circling the real problem, which is that **this is a threshold sentence**, and threshold sentences must do three things at once: name the action, name the outcome, name the feeling. "Press C" is the action. "Run a quick check / close out" is the outcome. The feeling is the one both of you forgot — the feeling is *completion*.

Try this:

> *"Press C to finish — I'll take one last look for anything rough, then we're done here."*

"One last look" is the threshold moment. "Anything rough" is the check. "Then we're done here" is the completion the user came for. It's 18 words, it names the action, it names what happens, and it gives the user the feeling of arriving somewhere.

And — here is where I become dangerous — I would like to note that **neither of my colleagues commented on the fact that 14 copies of this sentence is no longer the issue**. Under the revised plan, this sentence lives in ONE file. Which means *we can spend more care on it*. The elicitation pass didn't just save maintenance burden — it earned us the right to be precious about micro-copy in a way that would have been absurd when we were ctrl-c/ctrl-v'ing 14 times.

*[turns toward Armel]* So — which of the three phrasings feels like a door you want to walk through? Because I can defend all three, but only you know which one sounds like *your* product.

---

**Sophia asks: *Which phrasing feels right as the door you want to walk through?***

- Maya's: *"Press C to finish — I'll take a moment to check if anything felt rough, then we're done."*
- Caravaggio's: *"Press C to finish — I'll run a quick self-improvement check on this workflow, then close out."*
- Sophia's: *"Press C to finish — I'll take one last look for anything rough, then we're done here."*
- The current draft: *"Press C to finish and I'll do a quick check for any friction I hit in this workflow before closing out."*

_[Awaiting user response...]_

You can speak naturally with the agents — pick a phrasing, push back, or steer the conversation somewhere else entirely (the `skip_finish_gate` naming, the friction-vs-consistency trade-off in Decision 2, the Phase 0.5 pre-flight checks, or anywhere else in the plan).

`[E] Exit Party Mode — End the collaborative session`
